questions.question_text: Find the present worth of ₹ 930 due 3 years hence at 8% per annum. Also find the discount.
The true discount on a bill due 9 months hence at 12% per annum is ₹ 540. Find the amount of the bill and its present worth.
questions.solution:
Let amount be ₹ $x$. Then,
$\frac{x\times12\times\frac{3}{4}}{100+\left(12\times\frac{3}{4}\right)} = 540 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{540\times109}{9}\right) = ₹ 6540$.
$\therefore$ Amount = ₹ 6540.
P.W. = ₹ (6540 – 540) = ₹ 6000.

questions.question_text: The true discount on a certain sum of money due 3 years hence is ₹ 250 and the simple interest on the same sum for the same time and at the same rate is ₹ 375. Find the sum and the rate percent.
questions.solution:
T.D. = ₹ 250 and S.I. = ₹ 375.
$\therefore$ Sum due = ₹ $\left(\frac{S.I.\times T.D.}{(S.I.)-(T.D.)}\right) = ₹ \left(\frac{375\times250}{375-250}\right) = ₹ 750$.
Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times375}{750\times3}\right)\% = 16\frac{2}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: The difference between the simple interest and true discount on a certain sum of money for 6 months at $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ per annum is ₹ 25. Find the sum.
questions.solution:
Let the sum be ₹ $x$. Then,
T.D. = $\frac{x\times\frac{25}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}}{100+\left(\frac{25}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}\right)} = \left(x\times\frac{25}{4}\times\frac{4}{425}\right) = \frac{x}{17}$.
S.I. = $\left(x\times\frac{25}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{100}\right) = \frac{x}{16}$.
$\therefore \frac{x}{16} - \frac{x}{17} = 25 \Rightarrow 17x - 16x = 25 \times 16 \times 17 \Rightarrow x = 6800$.
Hence, sum due = ₹ 6800.

questions.question_text: A bill falls due in 1 year. The creditor agrees to accept immediate payment of the half and to defer the payment of the other half for 2 years. By this arrangement he gains ₹ 40. What is the amount of the bill, if the money be worth $12\frac{1}{2}\%$?
questions.solution:
Let the sum be ₹ $x$. Then,
$\left[\frac{x}{2} + \frac{\frac{x}{2}\times100}{100+\left(\frac{25}{2}\times2\right)}\right] - \left[\frac{x\times100}{100+\left(\frac{25}{2}\times1\right)}\right] = 40 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{2} + \frac{2x}{5} - \frac{8x}{9} = 40 \Rightarrow x = 3600$.
$\therefore$ Amount of the bill = ₹ 3600.

questions.question_text: The present worth of ₹ 2310 due $1\frac{1}{2}$ years hence, the rate of interest being 15% per annum, is
If the true discount on a sum due 2 years hence at 14% per annum be ₹ 168, the sum due is
The true discount on ₹ 2562 due 4 months hence is ₹ 122. The rate percent is
The true discount on ₹ 1760 due after a certain time at 12% per annum is ₹ 160. The time after which it is due is
questions.solution:
P.W. = ₹ (1760 – 160) = ₹ 1600.

∴ S.I. on ₹ 1600 at 12% = ₹ 160.

∴ Time = $\left(\frac{100\times160}{1600\times12}\right) = \frac{5}{6}$ years = $\left(\frac{5}{6}\times12\right)$ months = 10 months.

questions.question_text: The true discount on a bill due 9 months hence at 16% per annum is ₹ 189. The amount of the bill is
questions.solution:
Let P.W. be ₹ x.

Then, S.I. on ₹ x at 16% for 9 months = ₹ 189.

∴ $x\times16\times\frac{9}{12}\times\frac{1}{100} = 189$ or x = 1575.

∴ P.W. = ₹ 1575.

∴ Sum due = P.W. + T.D. = ₹ (1575 + 189) = ₹ 1764.

questions.question_text: The interest on ₹ 750 for 2 years is the same as the true discount on ₹ 960 due 2 years hence. If the rate of interest is the same in both cases, it is
questions.solution:
S.I. on ₹ 750 = T.D. on ₹ 960.

This means P.W. of ₹ 960 due 2 years hence is ₹ 750.

∴ T.D. = ₹ (960 – 750) = ₹ 210.

Thus, S.I. on ₹ 750 for 2 years is ₹ 210.

∴ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times210}{750\times2}\right)$% = 14%.

questions.question_text: The simple interest and the true discount on a certain sum for a given time and at a given rate are ₹ 85 and ₹ 80 respectively. The sum is
If ₹ 10 be allowed as true discount on a bill of ₹ 110 due at the end of a certain time, then the discount allowed on the same sum due at the end of double the time is
questions.solution:
S.I. on ₹ (110 – 10) for a certain time = ₹ 10.

S.I. on ₹ 100 for double the time = ₹ 20.

T.D. on ₹ 120 = ₹ (120 – 100) = ₹ 20.

T.D. on ₹ 110 = $\left(\frac{20}{120}\times110\right)$ = ₹ 18.33.

questions.question_text: A man wants to sell his scooter. There are two offers, one at ₹ 12,000 cash and the other at a credit of ₹ 12,880 to be paid after 8 months, money being at 18% per annum. Which is the better offer?
questions.solution:
P.W. of ₹ 12,880 due 8 months hence

= $\left[\frac{12880\times100}{100+(18\times\frac{8}{12})}\right] = \left(\frac{12880\times100}{112}\right)$ = ₹ 11500.

Clearly, ₹ 12,000 in cash is a better offer.

questions.question_text: Goods were bought for ₹ 600 and sold the same day for ₹ 688.50 at a credit of 9 months and thus gaining 2%. The rate of interest per annum is
The present worth of ₹ 1404 due in two equal half-yearly instalments at 8% per annum simple interest is
questions.solution:
Required sum = P.W. of ₹ 702 due 6 months hence + P.W. of ₹ 702 due 1 year hence

= $\left[\frac{100\times702}{100+(8\times\frac{1}{2})} + \frac{100\times702}{100+(8\times1)}\right] = ₹ (675 + 650) = ₹ 1325$.

questions.question_text: A trader owes a merchant ₹ 10,028 due 1 year hence. The trader wants to settle the account after 3 months. If the rate of interest is 12% per annum, how much cash should he pay?
questions.solution:
Required money = P.W. of ₹ 10028 due 9 months hence

= $\left[\frac{10028\times100}{100+(12\times\frac{9}{12})}\right] = ₹ 9200$.

questions.question_text: A man buys a watch for ₹ 1950 in cash and sells it for ₹ 2200 at a credit of 1 year. If the rate of interest is 10% per annum, the man
questions.solution:
S.P. = P.W. of ₹ 2200 due 1 year hence

= $\left[\frac{2200\times100}{100+(10\times1)}\right] = ₹ 2000$.

∴ Gain = ₹ (2000 – 1950) = ₹ 50.

questions.question_text: A man purchased a cow for ₹ 3000 and sold it the same day for ₹ 3600, allowing the buyer a credit of 2 years. If the rate of interest be 10% per annum, then the man has a gain of

A owes B, ₹ 1573 payable $1\frac{1}{2}$ years hence. Also B owes A, ₹ 1444.50 payable 6 months hence. If they want to settle the account forthwith, keeping 14% as the rate of interest, then who should pay and how much?
questions.solution:
A owes = P.W. of ₹ 1573 due $\frac{3}{2}$ years hence

= $\left[\frac{1573\times100}{100+(14\times\frac{3}{2})}\right] = ₹ 1300$.

B owes = P.W. of ₹ 1444.50 due 6 months hence

= $\left[\frac{1444.50\times100}{100+(14\times\frac{1}{2})}\right] = ₹ 1350$.

∴ B must pay ₹ 50 to A.

questions.question_text: A has to pay ₹ 220 to B after 1 year. B asks A to pay ₹ 110 in cash and defer the payment of ₹ 110 for 2 years. A agrees to it. If the rate of interest be 10% per annum, in this mode of payment
questions.option_a: There is no gain or loss to any one
questions.solution:
A has to pay = P.W. of ₹ 220 due 1 year hence

= $\left[\frac{220\times100}{100+(10\times1)}ight] = ₹ 200$.

A actually pays = ₹ 110 + P.W. of ₹ 110 due 2 years hence

= $\left[110 + \frac{110\times100}{100+(10\times2)}\right] = ₹ 192.66$.

∴ A gains = ₹ (200 – 192.66) = ₹ 7.34.

questions.question_text: ₹ 20 is the true discount on ₹ 260 due after a certain time. What will be the true discount on the same sum due after half of the former time, the rate of interest being the same?
questions.solution:
S.I. on ₹ (260 – 20) for a given time = ₹ 20.

S.I. on ₹ 240 for half the time = ₹ 10.

T.D. on ₹ 250 = ₹ 10.

∴ T.D. on ₹ 260 = $\left(\frac{10}{250}\times260\right)$ = ₹ 10.40.

questions.question_text: The true discount on a certain sum of money due $2\frac{2}{3}$ years hence, is ₹ 150 and the simple interest on the same sum for the same time and at the same rate is ₹ 200. Interest rate per annum is
questions.solution:
TD = ₹ 150 and SI = ₹ 200

Sum due = $\left(\frac{SI\times TD}{SI-TD}\right) = \left(\frac{200\times150}{200-150}\right) = ₹ 600$

Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times200}{600\times\frac{8}{3}}\right)$% = $\left(\frac{20000}{1600}\right)$% = $\left(\frac{200}{16}\right)$% = 12 $\frac{1}{2}$%.

questions.question_text: ₹ 20 is the true discount on ₹ 260 due after a certain time. What will be the true discount on the same sum due after half of the former time, the rate of interest being the same?
questions.solution:
SI on ₹ (260 – 20) for a given time = ₹ 20

SI on ₹ 240 for half the time = ₹ 20

TD on ₹ 250 = ₹ 10

TD on ₹ 260 = $\frac{10}{250}\times260$ = ₹ 10.40

questions.question_text: The total discount on ₹ 1860 due after a certain time at 5% is ₹ 60. Find the time after which it is due
The difference between simple interest and the true discount on ₹ 2400 due 4 years hence at 5% per annum simple interest is
questions.solution:
Given $A = 2400$, $R = 5\%$, $T = 4$ year
$\therefore TD = \frac{A\times R\times T}{100+R\times T}$
$= \frac{2400\times 4\times 5}{100+20} = ₹ 400$
$\therefore SI - TD = \frac{TD\times R\times T}{100}$
$= \frac{400\times 5\times 4}{100} = ₹ 80$
